title: AutoLogon enabled on device
description: Fires when the AutoLogon analyzer reports an active automatic sign-in (AutoAdminLogon or ForceAutoLogon set in Winlogon). AutoLogon can be a legitimate kiosk or shared-device configuration, but it is also a classic way to automate enrollment or OOBE, so an active AutoLogon during Autopilot is worth surfacing.
explanation:
This device has **automatic sign-in (AutoLogon) enabled** in the registry (`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon`). The `AutoAdminLogon` and/or `ForceAutoLogon` value is set, so Windows will log a user in without prompting for credentials.

This is legitimate for kiosk and shared-device scenarios, but it is also a common technique to automate enrollment or OOBE. If AutoLogon was not part of your intended deployment, treat it as a possible manipulation: review the configured default user, the source that wrote the keys (provisioning script, Win32 app, or the Sysinternals Autologon tool), and whether the account is more privileged than the kiosk use case requires.

**Sysinternals Autologon:** when the evidence includes `sysinternals_suspected`, the AutoLogon password is stored as an encrypted **LSA secret** instead of a plaintext `DefaultPassword` registry value — the signature of the Sysinternals Autologon tool (AutoLogon enabled, a default user set, and no registry password). That is a deliberate, tool-assisted setup: still legitimate for an approved kiosk, but a stronger indicator of intent than a flag left behind by accident (the rule raises its confidence accordingly).

Review the `autologon_analysis` event in the session timeline for the exact Winlogon values (default user / domain, AutoLogon count, whether a Sysinternals LSA-secret password is suspected).

remediation.title: Confirm AutoLogon is intended
steps: Open the autologon_analysis event in the session timeline and review the default user/domain and AutoLogon flags
Confirm whether AutoLogon is part of an approved kiosk / shared-device deployment
If unexpected, identify what wrote the Winlogon keys (provisioning script, Win32 app, or Sysinternals Autologon)

remediation.title: Disable AutoLogon if not required
steps: Remove DefaultPassword (and any Sysinternals LSA secret) if present
For kiosk scenarios, prefer Assigned Access / kiosk profiles over raw AutoLogon where possible

remediation.title: Run deliberate kiosks? Use the allow-list instead
steps: If AutoLogon is an approved kiosk configuration, configure the template rule ANALYZE-SEC-004 with your expected kiosk account name(s)
Then disable this rule (ANALYZE-SEC-002) so approved kiosk AutoLogon stays quiet while AutoLogon for unexpected users still warns

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft: Configure Windows to automatically sign in (AutoLogon)
